g.data-narration: Welcome to Simple Sampling of Attributes. This fundamental technique helps estimate population proportions from categorical data in computer science applications.
In computer science, we cannot always examine entire populations. Sampling allows us to make predictions about large datasets efficiently.
g.text: • Network traffic too large? Sample packets
• Software testing infinite? Sample test cases

g.data-narration: The population is the entire set of interest, while a sample is a subset we actually study to infer properties about the whole.
An attribute is a categorical characteristic we count, not measure. Examples include online/offline status, corrupted packets, or premium users.
g.text: A categorical characteristic that can be

g.data-narration: Population proportion p is the true unknown parameter. Sample proportion p-hat equals successes divided by sample size, estimating p.
When counting successes in independent trials with constant probability, the distribution follows the binomial model shown here.
g.text: For simple random sample counting x successes from n trials:

g.data-narration: The sampling distribution of p-hat has mean equal to p (making it unbiased) and standard error measuring estimate variability.
For large samples where np and n(1-p) both exceed 5, the binomial distribution approximates a normal distribution.
g.text: For large samples, p̂ is approximately normally distributed:

g.data-narration: The sampling distribution forms a bell curve centered at the true proportion p, with spread determined by the standard error.
Let's apply these concepts to estimate server uptime. We sample 500 random pings and count successful responses.
g.text: Scenario: You are a systems engineer. You cannot check continuously,
so you ping the server at random instances to estimate uptime.

g.data-narration: Of 500 pings, 485 received responses. We calculate p-hat as 485 divided by 500, giving us 0.97 or 97%.
We calculate standard error using p-hat as an estimate. This tells us the precision of our proportion estimate.
g.text: Since true p is unknown, use p̂ to estimate:

g.data-narration: Using the 95% confidence level with Z=1.96, we calculate the margin of error and construct the confidence interval.
The 95% confidence interval is 95.5% to 98.5%. We are confident the true server uptime proportion falls in this range.
Key takeaway: p-hat is an unbiased estimator of p. Larger samples reduce standard error, making our estimates more precise.
This technique applies to quality assurance, A/B testing, and estimating feature prevalence in software and user behavior analysis.
